coredata.dc:title: High performance electrochemical sensors for dopamine and epinephrine using nanocrystalline carbon quantum dots obtained under controlled chronoamperometric conditions
coredata.dc:creator: Canevari, Thiago C.
Nakamura, Marcelo
Cincotto, Fernando H.
de Melo, Fernando M.
Toma, Henrique E.
coredata.dc:description: This work describes a chronoamperometrically-controlled synthesis of nanocrystalline carbon quantum dots (C-dots) and their study based on UV-Vis, Raman, FTIR, fluorescence spectroscopy, atomic force and high resolution transmission electron microscopy. The electrochemical synthesis was carried out using 1-propanol as a carbon source and the C-dots properties exhibited a great dependence on the electrolysis time spent in the process. The C-dots were applied as prepared, on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode, showing excellent electrochemical response in the simultaneous determination of dopamine and epinephrine. The detection limits for the two analytes were 4.6 nmol L−1 and 6.1 nmol L−1, respectively, and the overall electrode performance exhibited a much better electrocatalytic response, in relation to the similar carbon modified electrodes prepared under comparable conditions, with single wall carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots Nanoparticles
Electrochemical synthesis
Electrocatalysis
Dopamine
Epinephrine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Simultaneous determination of dopamine and epinephrine by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV)
Comparison of the electrocatalytic response of GCE modified with nanocarbon allotropes for dopamine and epinephrine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of chronoamperometric synthesis of C-dots nanocrystalline using 1-propanol as carbon source.
Influence of the time of synthesis on the color and the particle size of C-dots.
High-resolution electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of the C-dots of as synthesized.
AFM topography images of C-dots 4.5 and C-dots 8.5 on mica substrates.
UV–vis spectra of C-dots 4.5 and C-dots 8.5.
PL spectra of (a) C-dots 4.5 and (b) C-dots 8.5. Excitation wavelengths varied from 365 to 485nm. Inset: UV–vis absorption spectrum (Abs) of C-dots 4.5 and C-dots 8.5.
FTIR spectra of C-dots as prepared in comparison with 1-propanol.
Raman spectrum of C-dots deposited on glass, λexc =532nm.

Electrocatalytical determination of dopamine (DA) and epinephrine (EP) (each C=5×10−6 molL−1) in PBS by GCE bare and GCE modified with C-dots 4.5 and C-dots 8.5. Measurements performed in pH 7.0 employing differential pulse voltammetry technique.
Simultaneous determination of epinephrine and dopamine using the a) GCE/C-dots 4.5 and b) GCE/C-dots 8.5, in PBS 7.0.

Comparative study of electrocatalytic response between CGE bare and GCE modified with single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), graphene oxide (GO) and C-dots 4.5 and C-dots 8.5 in the presence 8×10−6 molL−1 of dopamine and epinephrine.
Comparison between performance electrocatalytic of GCE/C-dots and others modified electrodes.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NiONP–MWCNT–DHP/GCE: glassy carbon electrode modified with nickel oxide nanoparticles and carbon nanotubes within a dihexadecylphosphate film; DPV: differential pulse voltammetry; DA: dopamine; EP: epinephrine; AgNP/SiO2/GO/GCE: mesoporous silica nanoparticles, graphene and silver nanoparticles; SWV: square-wave voltammetry; Poly(DA)-nanogold/GCE: polydopamine and gold nanoparticles; α-CD/CNT/GCE: α −cyclodextrin and carbon nanotubes; PAIUCPE: pre-anodized inlaying ultrathin carbon paste electrode; CV − cyclic voltammetry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of simultaneous determination of dopamine and epinephrine in human urine sample (GCE/C-dot 4.5).
Recovery of simultaneous determination of dopamine and epinephrine in human urine sample (GCE/C-dot 8.5).

head.ce:title: High performance electrochemical sensors for dopamine and epinephrine using nanocrystalline carbon quantum dots obtained under controlled chronoamperometric conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering School, Mackenzie Presbyterian University, 01302-907 São Paulo, SP, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, University of São Paulo, 05508-000, São Paulo, SP, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, University of São Paulo, 13560-970 São Carlos, SP, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work describes a chronoamperometrically-controlled synthesis of nanocrystalline carbon quantum dots (C-dots) and their study based on UV-Vis, Raman, FTIR, fluorescence spectroscopy, atomic force and high resolution transmission electron microscopy. The electrochemical synthesis was carried out using 1-propanol as a carbon source and the C-dots properties exhibited a great dependence on the electrolysis time spent in the process. The C-dots were applied as prepared, on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode, showing excellent electrochemical response in the simultaneous determination of dopamine and epinephrine. The detection limits for the two analytes were 4.6 nmol L−1 and 6.1 nmol L−1, respectively, and the overall electrode performance exhibited a much better electrocatalytic response, in relation to the similar carbon modified electrodes prepared under comparable conditions, with single wall carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon has a special relevance in nanotechnology because of its ability to form a large variety of nanomaterials, as exemplified by the carbon nanotubes, graphenes, fullerenes, carbon quantum dots and nanodiamonds. Such carbon-based materials are being explored in many different areas, due to their distinct properties, in association with their size, shapes and structures [1].

The main differences between these nanocarbon allotropes are the atomic arrangement presented by them: nanodiamonds have a 3D structure, graphene is 2D, carbon nanotube is 1D, while carbon quantum dots can be considered as 0D [2]. Among these carbon allotropes, carbon quantum dots are relatively new.

Carbon quantum dots, also known as C-dots consist of very small spherical nanoparticles (<10nm), wherein the carbon atoms are linked by a combination of sp2 and sp3 bonds. The major features presented by C-dots, turning them quite interesting materials, are their good photoluminescent properties, competing with the typical inorganic quantum dots [3], their low toxicity and high solubility in water. On the other hand, they also exhibit functional groups (particularly, carboxylate groups) suitable for applying surface modification procedures and their very small diameters can favor the appearance of quantum confinement effects [4,5].

C-dots are normally described as amorphous nanoparticles and have been obtained by employing different carbon sources and different synthetic routes [6]. Among the several methods of synthesis, the electrochemical ones are quite simple, requiring just a trivial chemical carbon source, working under alkaline conditions and using conventional electrodes (working, reference and auxiliary). In this case, however, the electrochemically generated C-dots can exhibit different characteristics, depending upon the experimental parameters involved, such as the applied voltage and current intensity, as well as the nature of the carbon source, the type of electrode and the processing time [7]. For this reason, the observed results cannot be easily predicted and a systematic study is required in order to understand the influence of the many variables and to keep improving the characteristics of the C-dots for their particular applications.

In this work, we have applied a chronoamperometrically controlled method in order to generate high performance C-dots displaying nanocrystalline features, luminescence and remarkable electrocatalytical activity in aqueous solution. They were successfully employed in the determination of dopamine (DA) and epinephrine (EP), typical neurotransmitters belonging to the class of catecholamines, considered of great relevance because of their essential roles in humans. As a matter of fact, their concentration levels should be frequently monitored in clinical analysis since they can be signaling the emergence of various diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, HIV, tumors, diabetes and cirrhosis [8–11].

Different analytical methods [12–14] have been employed for dopamine and epinephrine, however, their simultaneous determination is not trivial because their close similarity generates interference problems. However, when compared to other analytical techniques, the electrochemical method has the advantage of not requiring any specific pre-treatment of the samples, being practically applicable for the samples collected in situ. For this reason, the development of highly sensitive electrochemical sensors capable of determining both catecholamines simultaneously is of great analytical interest. Pursuing this relevant task, the electrochemically formed nanocrystalline C-dots were applied in electroanalytical sensors and the results are reported in this work.

All reagents were of high purity and used as supplied: 1-propanol (Losco Chemical), KOH (Synth), graphene oxide (GO) (Aldrich, CAS: 763713), single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) (Aldrich, CAS: 308068-56-6), Dopamine (Aldrich), Epinephrine (Aldrich). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1molL−1, pH 7.0) was prepared from NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4.

The modified electrodes were prepared by deposition of C-dots, SWCNTs and GO as thin films, on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Initially, the glassy carbon electrode was polished with alumina slurry, washed with distilled water and immersed in a beaker containing ethanol. After keeping for 5min in the ultrasound bath, the GCE was allowed to dry at room temperature and then its surface was modified with three different materials giving rise three different electrodes by the applying, independently, 9μL of 1-propanol suspensions containing C-dots as prepared, SWCNT (0.5mgmL−1) and GO (0.5mgmL−1). After this procedure, the electrodes were allowed to dry overnight at room temperature.

The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Hewlett-Packard HP-8453A diode array spectrophotometer using a quartz cuvette. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Turret 400 fluorometer. Raman measurements were performed using a WITec alpha-300R equipment, using an excitation laser with λ=532nm. The C-dot samples were directly prepared, by drop casting on a glass plate and allowed to dry at room temperature. FTIR spectra were recorded on a Bruker FTIR, ALPHA model spectrophotomer using the ATR module.

HR-TEM images were performed using a JEOL transmission electron microscope operating at 200kV. C-dots as prepared were deposited onto ultrathin carbon-coated copper grids. The AFM images were obtained using a PicoSPM I microscope attached to a Molecular Imaging Picoscan 2100 controller. For this purpose, the original C-dots suspension was diluted 100 times and deposited on atomic flat mica (Ted Pella Inc.). After drying at room temperature, the topographic images were acquired by Intermittent Contact AFM technique.

Electrochemical measurements were obtained using a potentiostat/galvanostat PGSTAT-20 and an electrochemical cell comprising a Ag/AgCl reference electrode, a platinum wire auxiliary electrode, and a glassy carbon electrode modified with the C-dots, SWCNTs or GO, as the working electrode. Differential pulse voltammetry technique (DPV) was used for electrochemical measurements of the studied analytes. The electrochemical measurements were performed in the presence of 10ml of PBS buffer solution, pH 7, with a scan rate of 10mVs−1.

The electrochemical formation of the C-dots involves the electrochemical oxidation of 1-propanol in highly basic medium (Fig. 1), at applied potentials high enough to generate hydroxyl radicals and carbonium ions [7,15,16].

For this reason, the mechanism involved should depend on the rates of electrochemical generation of such reactive species, justifying the use of a time controlled potential and currents based on chronoamperometry. Surprisingly, the C-dots obtained in this way were highly dispersed (Fig. 3) and of crystalline structure, with no evidence of agglomerates, in contrast to the preceding reports from the literature, using a different carbon source[7].

Typical high resolution electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of the C-dots are shown in Fig. 3 . The C-dots obtained after 4.5 and 8.5h exhibited a similar pattern, consistent with a spherical geometry with an average diameter of 3 and 4nm, respectively.
As exemplified in Fig. 3, the C-dots exhibit parallel lattice planes with a spacing distance around 0.2nm, consistent with the interlayer distance of graphitic (sp2) carbon.

The AFM images (Fig. 4 ), show that the C-dots 4.5 and 8.5 have spherical morphology and their typical heights of about 3.6nm and 6nm (supplementary data), respectively, are equivalent to 20-30 carbon or graphene layers.

The UV–vis spectrum of C-dots 4.5 and C-dots 8.5, shown in Fig. 5 , contrasts with those previously reported for C-dots generated from different techniques [7,17,18]. The observed bands at 228, 267, 325 and 375 (shoulder) nm can be tentatively ascribed to π−π* transitions associated with CC double bonds forming the carbon core, as well as to the π−π* and n−π* transitions involving CO and carboxylate functional groups at the surface of the C-dot [19]. Zhu et al. suggested that the absorption band around 267nm can be also be associated with excited defect states at the surface, induced by the presence of functional groups [20].

The absorption spectrum of C-dot 8.5 showed an increase in the intensity of absorption band around 228nm, reflecting the expansion of the conjugated bonds in the core. The increase of the absorption bands at 325 and 375nm can be related to the formation of the more functional and conjugated groups at the surface, or to the presence of C-dots of different sizes [21]. Therefore, the reaction time, keeping the potential and current fixed, can exert strong influences on the structure and functionality of the C-dots.

The small size presented by the C-dots promotes the emergence of quantum confinement effects as well as of special photoluminescent and electrochemical properties [2,22], as compared with other carbon allotropes.
As a matter of fact, both C-dots exhibit a characteristic luminescence spectrum when excited in the UV region (excitation wavelengths varied from 365 to 485nm.), as illustrated in Fig. 6 .

The photoluminescence spectra (PL) of C-dots 4.5 and 8.5 exhibit a slight shift from 450 to around 570cm−1 accompanying the increase of the nanoparticles size. The fluorescent properties can also be influenced by structural defects and the presence of functional groups promoting radiative recombination of surface-confined electrons and holes [23].

The FTIR spectra of the C-dots (Fig. 7 ) exhibit characteristic peaks, quite distinct from 1-propanol. The broad peak around 3250cm−1 is consistent with OH vibrational frequencies and is not accompanied by the CH peaks of the starting 1-propanol solvent. Therefore, it can be ascribed to the OH groups attached to the carbon atom. In principle, the FTIR peaks at 1659, 1570 and 1371cm−1 can arise from the CC vibrations of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, as well as from the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of the carboxylic groups, respectively [24]. It is important to note, that the C-dots obtained from 1-propanol, using the technique of chronoamperometry, after 4.5h and 8.5h, displayed a different spectra from those reported in the literature for distinct preparation procedures [7].

The Raman spectra of the C-dots exhibited four sharp peaks at 482, 1465, 1528 and 2437cm−1, as shown in Fig. 8 . There are also several less intense bands, presumably associated with the dried organic and inorganic material present in the electrochemical solution. Sharp Raman peaks are frequently observed in nanocarbon materials, involving the excitation of sp2 bonds [25,26] at the several π states. However, from the best of our knowledge, the observed Raman peaks cannot be associated with any previously reported nanocarbon material. However, it is interesting to note that fullerene, exhibits the two most intense bands at 486 an 1470cm−1, while graphene exhibits three sharp bands at 1350 (D band), 1583 (G band) and 2699cm−1 (2D band). In graphene, the D band around 1350cm−1 has been related to carbon structure defects (sp3 hybridization), and also to ring breathing modes; the G band at 1583cm−1 is related to the sp2 organized carbon structure of both rings and chains [24].
Up to the present time, in spite of the several reports on C-dots prepared in many different ways, there is no consense on their structure and properties.

The electrocatalytical response of GCE modified with C-dots 4.5 and C-dots 8.5, in the analytical determination of dopamine (DA) and epinephrine (EP) (each C=5×10−6 molL−1) can be seen in Fig. 9 . Both electrodes showed a much better electrocatalytic response (increase in current and potential shift to less positive values) for dopamine and epinephrine, in comparison with the bare GCE, exhibiting EDA =0.15V and EEP =0.32V, and EDA =0.16V and EEP =0.33V, respectively, while for bare GCE, EDA =0.22V and EEP =0.37V. The best response was observed for GCE/C-dots 4.5, presumably because of the larger active area associated with the smaller nanoparticles, favoring the electron-transfer kinetics at the electrode-solution interface.
The good separation between the potential responses for dopamine and epinephrine exhibited by both C-dots modified electrode, allows their simultaneous determination without interference problems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of dopamine and epinephrine by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 shows the DPV response for the simultaneous determination of epinephrine and dopamine using the GCE/C-dots 4.5 and GCE/C-dots 8.5, in PBS 7. The study was conducted by starting at 0.5V and performing the electrochemical reduction of the substrates, at variable concentrations in the range of 0.05 to 2.0μmolL−1. The electrochemical reduction has been preferred for monitoring, in order to prevent the interference from uric acid and ascorbic acid. In real samples, uric acid and ascorbic acid are critical interfering agents in the electrochemical determination of catecholamines [14]. For both electrodes, there is an increased electrocatalytic current, proportional to the dopamine and epinephrine concentrations.
In the case of GCE/C-dots 4.5 (inset Fig. 10a) the current vs concentration response is essentially linear up to 1μmolL−1, as expressed by equations:
and

The detection limit in the simultaneous determination of DA and EP, was calculated as recommended by IUPAC [27] using the 3σ/slope ratio, (where σ is the standard deviation in the mean current for 10 square wave voltammograms of the blank), and was respectively equal to 4.6 nmol L−1 and 6.1 nmol L−1. The detection limit also was calculated as recommended by IUPAC.
GCE/C-dots 8.5, showed a similar behavior the GCE/C-dots 4.5 electrode, and its electrochemical response can be expressed by
and

Their corresponding detection limits were 6.0 nmol L−1 and 6.5 nmol L−1. Taking into account that the concentration of DA and EP, in human urine of normal individuals [28] are 1.2 and 3.5μmolL−1, respectively, the proposed electrochemical sensor can be considered perfectly suitable for the determination of such species in real samples.

Both electrodes, GCE/C-dots 4.5 and GCE/C-dots 8.5, showed excellent electrocatalytic response for the simultaneous determination of dopamine and epinephrine. In addition, their detection limits proved to be better than other electrodes modified nanoparticles, GO, amino acids and quantum dots [14,29–31]. The good values of detection limit showed for GCE modified with C-dots can be due the high electroactive area, that is due the combination of small size of C-dots. The best electrocatalytic performance showed by GCE/C-dots compared with other modified electrodes is shown in Table 1 .

The good electrocatalytic response of modified GCE with C-dots may be due to the favorable characteristics provided by their nanocrystalline structure involving conducting C-sp2 bonds, and their very small diameters (3nm). The presence of functional groups, such as the carboxylic ones, is another relevant aspect for promoting a good interaction with the glassy carbon electrode and with the substrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the electrocatalytic response of GCE modified with nanocarbon allotropes for dopamine and epinephrine
ce:section.ce:para: For comparison purposes, a comparative study was performed for CGE modified with other nanocarbon allotropes, specifically, single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and graphene oxide (GO), prepared under identical conditions. As can been in Fig. 11 , GCE modified with C-dots exhibited a much better electrocatalytic activity as compared to the bare GCE, GCE/SWNCTs and GCE/GO systems. This study was performed in the presence 8×10−6 molL−1 of dopamine and epinephrine.

The modified electrode, GCE/C-dots, showed good reproducibility in its preparation and in its electrocatalytic response. In this study, 10 electrodes were prepared and the electrocatalytic response of ten electrodes were tested in the presence of dopamine and epinephrine, both with a concentration of 5×10−6 molL−1 in PBS, pH 7.0. The electrodes not showed significant loss of electrocatalytic activity, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3% and 3.5%, respectively. The modified electrodes showed high durability and was used for more than a month.

Ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) are the most probable biological interferents [36,37] in the determination of dopamine and epinephrine. As a matter of fact, the interference of these species in the simultaneous determination of dopamine and epinephrine has already been confirmed, and ascribed to their similar oxidation potentials [38]. In the present work, the measurements were performed starting from the concentration of dopamine and epinephrine fixed at 1.5×10−5 molL−1, using concentrations of UA and AA ten times higher. No electrocatalytic response has been found for UA and AA in the negative potential range studied (supplementary data). This is due to the fact that AA and AU exhibit only oxidative processes, not interfering in the simultaneous determination of DA and EP by a reduction mechanism. In addition, a better performance of the GCE/C-dots electrodes in relation to the other analytical methods [12,39] is implicit in their direct use in real samples, dispensing any previous treatment.

In order to test the versatility of GCE/C-dots electrode in the simultaneous determination of DA and EP in real samples, a study was conducted using the GCE/C-dots electrode in the evaluation of these species in urine. The high concentration of DA and EP excreted in the urine may be related to various health problems, such as Parkinson's disease, HIV, tumors, diabetes and cirrhosis [8–11], turning their determination in real samples an important point. In this study the simultaneous determination of DA and EP were performed in urine sample obtained from a healthy volunteer. The quantification of DA and EP concentrations were performed in triplicate, without any pre-treatment procedure, using the standard addition method. The amounts of DA and EA in the human spiked urine samples were evaluated by the calibration method using the GCE/C-dots 4.5 and GCE/C-dots 8.5 electrodes and the results are summarized in Table 2 and Table 3 . As one can see, the proposed analytical method is highly effective. The fact that no electrocatalytic signal related to electrochemical reduction of DA and EP in urine could be found in the starting heathy volunteer sample, imply that their concentrations were below the detection limit of the electrode.

The C-dots obtained by the chronoamperometric method, exhibited crystalline structure and diameters smaller than 3.5 nanometers, as evidenced by the HR-TEM micrographs. Their small size combined with Csp2 and presence of functional groups, especially carboxylic groups, imparted a good electrocatalytic response in the simultaneous determination of dopamine and epinephrine. In addition, the C-dots were highly stable and luminescent in solution, opening exciting perspectives for developing optical devices and electrochemical sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are also grateful to CNPq (150503/2015-6) and FAPESP (2013/24725-4) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2016.05.108.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
